schema.appDescription: See how inflation erodes the purchasing power of your retirement corpus over time. Calculate real vs nominal value of your savings 10, 20, or 30 years from now.

howToSteps.name: Enter your details
howToSteps.text: Fill in the input fields for the Inflation Impact Calculator.

howToSteps.name: View instant results
howToSteps.text: Results update in real time as you type.

howToSteps.name: Explore breakdown
howToSteps.text: Open the year-by-year breakdown to see numbers over time.

howToSteps.name: Compare scenarios
howToSteps.text: Adjust inputs to compare scenarios and optimise your decision.

faq.q: How does inflation affect retirement savings?
faq.a: ₹1 crore today at 6% inflation is worth only ₹31.2 lakh in real terms after 20 years. This means your retirement corpus must more than triple just to maintain purchasing power. Investments that don't beat inflation by a significant margin are actually losing value in real terms.

faq.q: What is the average inflation rate in India?
faq.a: India's CPI inflation has averaged 6–7% over the past decade (2013–2024). Food inflation is typically higher. For retirement planning, use 6–7% as a conservative assumption. Healthcare inflation is higher (10–12%) — budget separately for medical costs in retirement.

faq.q: How can I protect my retirement corpus from inflation?
faq.a: Invest in assets that beat inflation: equity mutual funds (12–15% historical CAGR), equity-linked NPS (10–12%), real estate (7–10%). Even in retirement, maintain 30–40% equity exposure to beat inflation over a 25-30 year retirement horizon. Avoid parking all corpus in FDs post-retirement.
No. Calculations run in your browser.
Yes. Works on all modern smartphones.

examples.heading: ₹50 lakh savings — purchasing power in 20 years
examples.text: ₹50 lakh at 6% inflation: real value in 20 years = ₹15.6 lakh. You need ₹50 lakh to buy the same goods that ₹15.6 lakh buys today. This is why FDs at 7.1% barely beat 6% inflation — you're only growing wealth at 1% real rate.

examples.heading: Required return to maintain purchasing power
examples.text: To maintain ₹50 lakh in real terms over 20 years, you need 6% return just to break even (same as inflation). To actually grow by 5% real, you need 11% nominal return. This is why equity (historical 12–15% CAGR) is essential in long-term portfolios.
